---
title: "Generalized Ideal Point Models for Noisy Dynamic Measures in the Social Science"
subtitle: "American Political Science Association"
date: 2025-09-13
date-format: long
incremental: true
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: dark
    incremental: true
    embed-resources: true
    slideNumber: false
    self-contained: false
#    mermaid-format: svg
    mermaid:
      theme: dark
author: 
  - name: Robert Kubinec
    affiliation: University of South Carolina
    corresponding: true
indent: true
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
css: custom.css
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false

library(patchwork)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)


```

## Measurement Error Is Everywhere

![](measurement_meme.jpg)

## Some People Believe Otherwise

![](rabois.png)

## `idealstan` Is a General Purpose Measurement Tool

Main features:

1.  Diverse time series processes.
2.  Non-ignorable missing data.
3.  Mixed discrete & continuous data.
4.  Map-reduce for big data.
5.  Ideal point marginal effects.

## Model Specification

For each person $i$ and indicator/item $j$,

$$
\Large \class{fragment}{\underset{\text{Data}}{Y_{ij}}} = g\left(\class{fragment}{\overset{\text{Latent Trait}}{\alpha_i}}\class{fragment}{\underset{\text{Discrimination}}{\gamma_j}} - \class{fragment}{\overset{\text{Difficulty}}{\beta_j}}\right)
$$

## Model Specification

$$
\Large \underset{\text{Data}}{Y_{ij}} = g\left(\overset{\text{Latent Trait}}{\alpha_i}\underset{\color{red}{Discrimination}}{\color{red}{\gamma_j}} - \overset{\text{Difficulty}}{\beta_j}\right)
$$

1.  If [discrimination]{style="color: red;"} is positive, latent trait *positively* predicts the indicator/item.
2.  If [discrimination]{style="color: red;"} is negative, latent trait *negatively* predicts the indicator/item.

## Priors

For a given item $j$ and person $i$,

\begin{align}
\alpha_i &\sim \text{Normal}(0,3)\\
\gamma_j &\sim \text{GeneralizedBeta}(2,2)\\
\beta_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0,3)
\end{align}\label{eq-genprior}

## Missing Data Formulation

For an item $j$, person $i$, and missingness indicator $r$,

$$
    \prod^{I}_{i=1} \prod^{J}_{j=1}
    \begin{cases}
    \zeta(\alpha_{i}'\nu_j - \omega_j ) & \text{if } r=0, \text{ and} \\
    (1-\zeta({\alpha_{i}'\nu_j - \omega_j}))L(Y_{ijr}|\alpha_i,\gamma_j,\beta_j) & \text{if } r=1
    \end{cases}
$$ {#eq-inflate2}

## Time Series: Random Walk

For a given person $i$ and time point $t$,

$$
\alpha_{it} \sim N(\delta_i+ \alpha_{it-1},\sigma_i)
$$ {#eq-rwc}

## Time Series: AR(1)

For a given person $i$ and time point $t$,

$$
\alpha_{it} = \delta_i + \psi_i\alpha_{it-1} + \sigma_i\epsilon_{it}
$$ {#eq-ar}

## Time Series: Gaussian Process

For a given person $i$ and time point $t$,

$$
f(x_t) \sim N(\mu(f(x_t)),\Sigma(f(x_t)))
$$ {#eq-gp1}

## Time Series: Spline

For a given person $i$ and time point $t$,

$$
S_{q,d}(t) = B_{s,d}(t)A_i
$$ {#eq-splinefunc}

## Parallelization

1.  Markov Chains can't be parallelized.
2.  Gradient calculations for Hamiltonians can.
3.  `idealstan` allows for within-memory within-chain parallelization by items/persons.

## Variational Inference

`idealstan` contains 2 variational algorithms:

1.  Pathfinder
2.  Laplace approximation

## Simulation

1.  Compare `idealstan` to other dynamic packages (`emIRT`, `mcmcPACK`, `dwnominate`).
2.  Measure fidelity to "true" ideal points and ability to capture downstream regression.
3.  Vary missing data & time series process.

## Results: True Ideal Point Correlation

```{r}
kendall_plot <- readRDS("data/kendall_tau.rds")

kendall_plot + theme(text=element_text(size=14),
                  axis.text=element_text(size=14))
```

## Results: S Error Rates

```{r}

s_err_plot <- readRDS("data/s_errors.rds")

s_err_plot + theme(text=element_text(size=14),
                  axis.text=element_text(size=14))

```

## Results: Parallelization with Multiple Cores

```{r}

time_plot <- readRDS("data/plot_time_elapsed_n_items.rds")

time_plot + theme(text=element_text(size=14),
                  axis.text=element_text(size=14))

```

## Empirical: Justin Amash in the 115th Congress

```{r}

un1_a <- readRDS("data/un1_a.rds")
  un2_a <- readRDS("data/un2_a.rds")
  un3_a <- readRDS("data/un3_a.rds")
  gp_a <- readRDS("data/gp_a.rds")
  rw_a <- readRDS("data/rw_a.rds")
  ar_a <- readRDS("data/ar_a.rds")

un1_a + un2_a + un3_a + ar_a + gp_a + rw_a + plot_layout(nrow=2,
                                             guides="collect") + 
  plot_annotation(caption="Posterior average values shown for 115th Congress legislator ideal points.\nItem discrimination for Republican party-line votes was constrained to be positive.") & theme(legend.position = "top",
                                                                                                                                                                                                    plot.title=element_text(size=14)) + theme(strip.text = element_text(size=14))

```

## Empirical: Tim Walz 4th Quarter Blitz

```{r}

  un1_tw <- readRDS("data/un1_tw.rds")
  un2_tw <- readRDS("data/un2_tw.rds")
  un3_tw <- readRDS("data/un3_tw.rds")
  gp_tw <- readRDS("data/gp_tw.rds")
  rw_tw <- readRDS("data/rw_tw.rds")
  ar_tw <- readRDS("data/ar_tw.rds")

un1_tw + un2_tw + un3_tw + ar_tw + gp_tw + rw_tw + plot_layout(nrow=2,
                                             guides="collect") + 
  plot_annotation(caption="Posterior average values shown for 115th Congress legislator ideal points.\nItem discrimination for Republican party-line votes was constrained to be positive.") & theme(legend.position = "top",
                                                                                                                                                                                                    plot.title=element_text(size=14))

```

## Empirical: 1990 - 2018 Monthly Congressional Ideal Points

```{r}

# load all these distributions, plot them 

all_group_big <- lapply(list.files("data/","idealpts",full.names = T), 
                        readRDS) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id="model") %>% 
  mutate(Spline=case_match(as.character(model),
                          c("1","2")~"2°",
                          c("3","4")~"3°",
                          c("5","6")~"4°"),
         Type=case_match(model,
                          c("2","4","6")~"With Abstentions",
                          c("1","3","5")~"Only Observed\nVotes"))

# make labels 

annotations <- tibble(x=rep(c(ymd("1993-01-01"),ymd("1993-01-01")),2),
           y=rep(c(-7,7),2),
           label=rep(c("Democrats","Republicans"),2),
           Type=rep(unique(all_group_big$Type),each=2))

all_group_big %>% 
  ggplot(aes(y=mean_est,x=Time_Point)) +
  geom_line(aes(linetype=Spline,colour=Group)) +
  facet_wrap(~Type,nrow=2) + 
  ggthemes::theme_clean() +
  scale_color_manual(values=c(R="red",
                              D="blue"),na.translate=F,name="") + 
  guides(color="none",
         linetype=guide_legend(override.aes = list(color = "black"))) + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels=c("More\nLiberal","-5","0","5","More\nConservative"),breaks=c(-10,-5,0,5,10)) +
  geom_text(data=annotations,aes(label=label,x=x,y=y),colour="black") +
  labs(y="Ideal Point",x="",
       caption=stringr::str_wrap("Plot shows party-average monthly ideal points for the U.S. House from 1990 to 2018 that are aggregated from individual legislator trajectories. Ideal point is derived from a Bayesian IRT model fit with idealstan. Missing-data models account for strategic abstention while observed models only use recorded votes. Each line is an estimate from a different type of spline model in terms of degrees with knots for each presidential administration. Discrete jumps in the time series show periods when new legislators entered."))  + theme(text=element_text(size=14),
                  axis.text=element_text(size=14))


```

# Appendix

## LLM Performance

![](images/upset_plot-06.png)

## Retweet Counts from Tunisia and Egypt in 2013 {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](retweets_counts_white.png)

## Latent Scales: Secularism vs. Islamism {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](d1_plot_white.png)

## One-way Censoring Among Islamist Tweeters {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

![](d1_discrim_white.png)

## What Does HMC Look Like

<https://chi-feng.github.io/mcmc-demo/app.html?algorithm=HamiltonianMC&target=banana>

## Big Online Polls Over-estimated COVID-19 Vaccine Uptake

![](images/covid.png){fig-align="center" width="1000px"}

## The Science of Measurement

![](mycity_screwup.png)

## Asahi-Todai Survey Estimates

![](images/dark_mode_asahi_plot-01.png)

## Missingness Discrimination

![](images/missingness_discrim_dark.png)

## Ideal Point Marginal Effects

For each item $j$, person $i$, and external covariate $x$,

$$
\frac{\partial Y_{ijtm}}{\partial x} \left( L_m(\gamma_j(\alpha_{it} + \phi x) - \beta_j) \right) = \phi \gamma_j L_m'(\gamma_j(\alpha_{it} + \phi x) - \beta_j)
$$ {#eq-deriv}

## Marginal Effect of Unemployment on U.S. Congress Vote

![](images/idealpt_marg_eff_dark-02.png)
